org_details: {"accronyms":"MECOMHEDI","yearsOfRegistration":"7","lga":"1","state":"1","cac":"1","otherRegistration":"1","other":"","headOfficeState":"Borno","officeAddress":"NO 389 Gama street behind Meltdew gas station Pompomari by-pass, Maiduguri Borno state","emailAddress":"info@mecomhedi.org","facebook":"https:\/\/www.facebook.com\/profile.php?id=61553060055016&mibextid=LQQJ4d","twitter":"https:\/\/twitter.com\/mecomhedi","linkedIn":"https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/company\/mecomhedi\/","orgMission":"With an unwavering passionate commitment to empowering humanity through the transformative power of media and communication, we serve as a beacon where media communication becomes a force for positive change, bringing attention to humanitarian crises and advocating for sustainable development, in collaboration with other stakeholders to foster positive change, inspire empathy, and promote healthier living across borders, cultures, and communities","orgVision":"We aspire to be at the forefront of media-driven humanitarian initiatives that harnesses the power of media to raise awareness inspire action and bring about lasting change in the lives of individuals and communities facing adversity.","website":"Mecomhedi.org","thematicAreas":null}
org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"","annualReports":"Project Report Summary MECOMHEDI has implemented projects targeted at creating awareness and sensitizing the general community on measure to prevent the spread of child killer communicable disease such as Measles, Yellow fever, Malaria, cholera and COVID-19, funded by UNICEF and the Borno State Primary Health Care Development Agency (SPHCDA) from 21st September to 21st October 2021. In 2022 the organization implemented a peace building twin project, \u201cOutreach Campaign to Promote Tolerance and Counter Violent Extremism Messaging and Misinformation\u201d and \u201cOutreach Campaign to Counter Violent Extremism Messaging and Misinformation\u201d, funded by USAID through the Nigerian Northeast Connection (NNEC) from July 2022 to December 2022. The project was implemented in five LGAs of Shani, Kwaya-kusar, Hawul, Jere and MMC, in Borno state. MECOMHEDI has subsequently implemented series of sensitization programs in Yobe and Adamawa states respectively. In 2023, MECOHEDI through the Borno State Primary Health Care Development Agency implemented a UNICEF funded campaign radio program project on sensitization of maternal and neonatal tetanus vaccination for caregivers of child bearing age in MMC and JERE local governments reaching out to an estimated 3 million persons through four different radio channels, airing of jingles and live phone -in discussion sessions. The organization is currently implementing a self-funded multi-sectoral Health, Nutrition, Protection, WaSH and Education project targeting Gajiganna and Tungushe communities of Magumeri LGA, Borno State. Project Title: Awareness And Sensitizing The General Community On Measure To Prevent The Spread Of Child Killer Communicable (Funded Unicef through Primary Health Care Development Agency) Project Duration: 21st September to 21st October 2021. Project Summary After the lockdown because of COVID 19 many people thought there was no need for using face mask because according to them there was no more corona disease and the use of face mask was neglected in Borno state. With the support of UNICEF, Media Communication for Health and Development Initiative (MECOHEDI) embarked on live radio discussion programs in harmony FM BIU covering BIU and HAWUL axis reaching estimated target of about 600 to 800 people also jingles were produced in Hausa, Kanuri, Babur bura and English. Skits was also produced in Hausa, Kanuri, Babur bura and English. As part of awareness creations, the jingles were aired on two radio stations namely: Borno radio and peace FM in the above mentioned languages for 30 days each six (6) slots daily. Live discussion program was done on harmony FM BIU with 40 participants from the LGA which was aired including repeats. Harmony FM has covered listeners in BIU LGA, HAWUL LGA and part of SHANI LGA. The live discussion program was on the importance of using face masks and hand washing with soap under running water at all times, and how to protect themselves from being contacted with the corona virus. An estimated phone calls range between 6 to 7 in each programme. The jingles aired on Borno Radio and PEACE FM covered MMC, JERE, MAFA, MAGUMERI, KONDUGA, GUBIO and some parts of southern Borno. Project Outcome \u2022 Increased Awareness and Behavioral Change: The face mask awareness program in Borno State, spearheaded by Media Communication for Health and Development Initiative (MECOHEDI) in collaboration with UNICEF, has achieved remarkable success in increasing public awareness and fostering positive behavioral change. Through live radio discussions, jingles, and skits in multiple languages, the initiative effectively conveyed the importance of using face masks and practicing proper hand hygiene. The program\\\\\\'s success is evident in the active participation of approximately 40 individuals from the Local Government Area (LGA) in the live discussions on Harmony FM BIU. The engagement of the community members reflects a tangible shift in attitudes towards preventive measures, indicating a positive response to the campaign. \u2022 Broad Geographical Coverage and Inclusivity: The strategic selection of radio stations and the production of content in diverse languages contributed to the broad geographical coverage of the awareness campaign. Harmony FM BIU reached BIU LGA, HAWUL LGA, and parts of SHANI LGA, while jingles on Borno Radio and Peace FM extended the impact to MMC, JERE, MAFA, MAGUMERI, KONDUGA, GUBIO, and southern Borno. This inclusive approach ensured that the message transcended cultural and linguistic barriers, effectively resonating with a diverse audience. The success of the program lies in its ability to tailor communication to the unique needs of each community, fostering a sense of ownership and understanding. \u2022 Active Listener Engagement and Participation: The live radio discussions on Harmony FM BIU not only disseminated crucial information but also facilitated direct interaction with the community through an estimated 6 to 7 phone calls during each program. This active listener engagement indicates that the audience not only absorbed the information but also sought clarification and additional insights. The two-way communication established during these programs is a testament to the success of the initiative in creating a dialogue and addressing community concerns, thereby strengthening the impact of the awareness campaign. \u2022 Sustainable Impact and Ongoing Efforts: The month-long airtime for jingles on Borno Radio and Peace FM has contributed to the sustainability of the program\\\\\\'s impact. By reinforcing the message consistently over a prolonged period, the initiative has laid the groundwork for lasting behavioral change. Additionally, the success of this campaign provides a foundation for future endeavors in public health awareness. The collaboration between MECOHEDI and UNICEF has not only addressed the immediate challenge of post-lockdown complacency but has also established a model for proactive and community-centric health communication, ensuring ongoing success in the fight against COVID-19 and other public health concerns. TOTAL OF 720 SLOTS WAS AIRED \uf0d8 Borno Radio 360 slots \uf0d8 Peace FM 360 slots LANGUAGES USED IN AIRING THE PROGRAMMES The languages used in airing the jingles were Hausa, Babur Bura, Kanuri and English. MOTORIZED CAMPAIGN IN MAGUMERI Motorized campaign in Magumeri LGA for the period of 26 days using two 2 hired vehicles\/PAS. The motorized campaign was done in Kanuri and Hausa for the benefit of people to understand the message in local languages. TARGET AUDIENCE All the jingles, live discussions and motorized were targeted towards the public. Project Title: Outreach Campaigns to Promote Tolerance and Counter Violent Extremism Messaging and Misinformation Project Duration: July \u2013 December, 2022 Project Summary In response to the urgent need for peace-building efforts in Northeast Nigeria, with from funding from United State Agency for International Development (USAID) through the Nigerian Northeast Connection, Media Communication for Health and Development Initiative implemented an outreach campaign to promote tolerance and counter extremist violence and misinformation in 5 local governments of Borno state. The project seeks to address misinformation, foster community engagement and discussion, and empower community structure, including women, men, adolescents, and youths in fostering peace and co-existence in Shani, Kwaya-kusar, Hawul, Jere, and MMC local government areas. Through strategic outreach efforts in five LGAs, the project laid the groundwork for sustainable positive change and contributed to building resilient communities resistant to the influence of extremist messaging. The impact of this initiative resonates in the enhanced social cohesion and understanding among diverse community members in Borno State. In an effort to promote peace and mitigate misinformation and violent extremist, the project was extended by 2 months. Project Impact: \u2022 Community Engagement: The project successfully engaged with the target population, encouraging open dialogue and promoting understanding among community members. \u2022 Misinformation Mitigation: By addressing misinformation related to violent extremism, the campaign contributed to reducing the impact of false narratives and fostering a more informed community. \u2022 Empowering Youths: Involving adolescents and youths in the initiative empowered them as active contributors to peace-building efforts, establishing a foundation for positive social change. Project Title: Awareness and Sensitization Campaign on Maternal and Neonatal Tetanus Funding Source: Funded by UNICEF Through Borno State Primary Health Care Development Agency Project Duration: February 2023. Project Summary: Ensuring the widespread awareness and adherence to tetanus vaccination is paramount in the prevention of maternal and neonatal tetanus. Recognizing this, the project emphasizes the need to refocus efforts on existing preventive strategies, concurrently exploring innovative approaches like the slow-release mono-dose tetanus vaccine and integrating tetanus prevention into school health programs. Additionally, the project advocates for political commitment to sustain immunization programs for women of child-bearing age. Implementation Summary: Media Communication for Health and Development Initiative (MECOHEDI) executed an impactful awareness and sensitization campaign on maternal and neonatal tetanus, generously funded by UNICEF through the Borno State Primary Health Care Development Agency. The project unfolded through strategically designed radio discussion program sessions and the dissemination of jingles in two local languages, namely Hausa and Kanuri. The targeted areas encompassed three local governments: MMC, Jere, and Konduga, within Borno State. The campaign was executed with precision, spanning the entire month of February 2023. This initiative signifies a concerted effort to bridge information gaps and promote preventive measures against maternal and neonatal tetanus, aligning with global health objectives. By leveraging effective communication channels and local languages, MECOHEDI, with the support of UNICEF, has taken a proactive step towards ensuring the well-being of mothers and newborns in Borno State. The project\\\\\\'s comprehensive approach, encompassing radio discussions and jingles, reflects a commitment to reaching diverse audiences and fostering sustained awareness on the importance of tetanus vaccination in the specified localities.","auditedAccounts":"","keyEvaluations":""}

org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Strategic Goals: Expand Access to Educational Resources Establish learning centers in underserved communities Provide free or low-cost access to educational materials, including books, digital resources, and hands-on learning kits Offer tutoring and mentorship programs to support academic achievement Integrate Technology and Digital Literacy Equip learning centers with modern technology, such as computers, tablets, and coding equipment Develop technology-based learning programs to teach digital skills, coding, and computer science Collaborate with tech companies and industry experts to provide training and workshops Cultivate Cognitive and Social Development Implement play-based learning activities that stimulate critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity Facilitate group activities and workshops to foster social skills, teamwork, and emotional intelligence Provide counseling and mental health support services to address the unique challenges faced by the community Empower Self-Directed Learning Create flexible, student-centered learning environments that encourage exploration and self-discovery Offer workshops and training to help participants identify and develop their hidden talents and interests Provide guidance and resources to support participants in pursuing their passions and goals Strengthen Community Partnerships and Outreach Collaborate with local schools, community organizations, and government agencies to identify and reach underserved populations Engage with community leaders and stakeholders to understand the unique needs and challenges of the target population Develop a robust marketing and communication strategy to raise awareness and attract participants to the program Key Performance Indicators: Number of learning centers established Percentage of participants who demonstrate improved academic performance Percentage of participants who acquire new digital skills and certifications Percentage of participants who report increased confidence, self-awareness, and social skills Percentage of participants who pursue their identified talents and interests Number of community partnerships and outreach initiatives Funding and Resources: Secure funding through grants, corporate sponsorships, and individual donations Leverage in-kind donations of technology, educational materials, and volunteer support Explore revenue-generating activities, such as fee-based workshops or consulting services","annualReports":"NA","auditedAccounts":"NA","keyEvaluations":""}

org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Strategic Plan 2021-2025: Beraka Better Life Initiative (BBLI) Vision: Unifying and advancing global aid in creating a better life. Mission: To promote the rights, well-being, and improve the quality of lives through the provision of accessible healthcare, education, and diverse innovative solutions to vulnerable and underprivileged children, youths, and women. Core Values: Diversity, Love, Integrity, Family, Excellence (DLIFE). 1. Goal: Enhance Program Impact and Reach Objectives: \u2022 Increase the number of children, youth, and women beneficiaries served annually by 25%. \u2022 Expand program operational areas to reach underserved communities, particularly in rural areas. \u2022 Develop partnerships with local organizations and community leaders to amplify program impact. Strategies: \u2022 Conduct regular needs assessments within target communities to identify specific health, education, and empowerment needs of children, youth, and women. \u2022 Collaborate with local NGOs, government agencies, and community leaders to leverage resources and expertise in program implementation. \u2022 Implement monitoring and evaluation systems to track program outcomes and ensure accountability. 2. Goal: Strengthen Health Education and Promotion Objectives: \u2022 Provide comprehensive health education programs for children, youth, and women on topics such as nutrition, hygiene, and disease prevention. \u2022 Increase access to healthcare services through mobile clinics and community health outreach initiatives. \u2022 Advocate for policies that prioritize healthcare access and education for vulnerable populations. Strategies: \u2022 Develop culturally sensitive health education materials and resources tailored to the needs of children, youth, and women. \u2022 Collaborate with healthcare professionals and local health authorities to conduct health screenings, immunizations, and awareness campaigns. \u2022 Engage in advocacy efforts to promote policies that support healthcare access and education for vulnerable populations. 3. Goal: Empower Children, Youth, and Women Through Education Objectives: \u2022 Provide quality education programs for children, youth, and women, focusing on literacy, numeracy, and life skills development. \u2022 Increase access to educational resources and infrastructure, particularly in rural and underserved areas. \u2022 Advocate for policies that prioritize education access and quality for children, youth, and women. Strategies: \u2022 Establish community learning centers and libraries to provide educational resources and support to children, youth, and women. \u2022 Provide scholarships, mentorship programs, and vocational training opportunities to empower youth and women in pursuing their educational and career goals. \u2022 Engage in advocacy efforts to promote policies that address barriers to education access and quality, particularly for marginalized populations. 4. Goal: Promote Economic Empowerment and Gender Equality Objectives: \u2022 Provide skill acquisition training, entrepreneurship opportunities, and microfinance support for youth and women. \u2022 Increase access to financial literacy education and resources to promote economic empowerment and independence. \u2022 Advocate for policies that promote gender equality and women\\'s economic participation. Strategies: \u2022 Develop entrepreneurship and vocational training programs tailored to the needs and interests of youth and women. \u2022 Establish microfinance initiatives and savings groups to provide financial support and resources for income-generating activities. \u2022 Engage in advocacy efforts to address gender disparities in access to economic opportunities and resources. Monitoring and Evaluation: \u2022 Establish a robust monitoring and evaluation framework to track progress towards strategic goals and objectives. \u2022 Conduct regular reviews and assessments to measure program effectiveness and identify areas for improvement. \u2022 Utilize feedback from beneficiaries, stakeholders, and staff to inform decision-making and ensure accountability. Conclusion: This strategic plan outlines BBLI\\'s commitment to targeting children, youth, and women in the thematic areas of health, education, and empowerment over the next five years. Through strategic partnerships, advocacy efforts, and a commitment to excellence, BBLI aims to create positive change and improve the lives of vulnerable populations in Sokoto State and beyond.","annualReports":"","auditedAccounts":"","keyEvaluations":""}

Leveraging Technological Solutions 6. Finance 7. Procurement","partners":"Absolutely CODERHI is always open to partnerships that align with our mission and objectives. We welcome collaboration in various sectoral areas to enhance our impact and reach. Here are some specific areas where we are seeking partnerships and the corresponding activities: 1. Health Sector: We are interested in partnering with healthcare providers, NGOs, and government agencies to expand access to sexual and reproductive health services, conduct health education workshops, and implement HIV\/AIDS prevention and awareness campaigns. 2. Community Development Collaboration with organizations focused on community development is vital for us. We aim to work together on participatory development planning, inclusive governance initiatives, and capacity-building programs for community members. 3. Women\\\\\\'s Empowerment: Partnerships with women-focused organizations are essential for promoting gender equity, poverty reduction, and skills development among women. We seek to establish skills acquisition centers, provide support for livelihood initiatives, and promote parent-child communication on health and social issues. 4. Capacity Building and Collaboration: We are interested in collaborating with institutions and experts to build the capacity of individuals in areas such as health education, community development planning, and inclusive governance practices. Training workshops, seminars, and knowledge-sharing initiatives are areas of potential collaboration. 5. Livelihood Support: Partnerships with organizations providing livelihood support can help us empower vulnerable groups, especially women, through the provision of soft loans, skills training, and income-generating activities."}

org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Strategic Plan: 1. Needs Assessment and Program Development: \u2022 Conduct a comprehensive needs assessment to identify the specific challenges and needs of vulnerable youth, orphans, the girl-child, and women in the community. \u2022 Develop tailored programs and initiatives focusing on education, vocational training, life skills development, entrepreneurship, and psychosocial support to address identified needs. \u2022 Offer mentorship programs and career counseling to guide youth, orphans, and women in making informed decisions about their future. \u2022 Establish monitoring and evaluation mechanisms to assess the effectiveness and impact of programs in achieving desired outcomes. \u2022 Develop a sustainability plan to ensure the long-term viability of the organization\u2019s programs and initiatives, including diversified funding sources, income-generating activities, and strategic partnerships. \u2022 Invest in capacity building initiatives to strengthen the skills and knowledge of staff, volunteers, and community members involved in program delivery and management.","annualReports":"","auditedAccounts":"","keyEvaluations":""}

org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Executive Summary: ORGANIZATIONAL INFORMATION: 1. Name: Dakkedan Education Development And Humanitarian Support Association (Founder Lawal Danhalilu Memorial Community Secondary School). No. 57 Maiduguri Road, Dutsin Amare Quarters Katsina, Katsina State Nigeria Established in the year 2015 2. Complete Constitution as well as Information on the Organizational goals and services : CONSTITUTION OF DAKKEDAN (Acronym) Education Development and Humanitarian Support Association DUTSIN-AMARE - K\/MARUSA-KERAU \u2013DANTURA (meaning) KATSINA 2015 PREAMBLE The constitution of this very Association was formulated to direct, assist and governs the affairs of the community. The constitution is expressed in articulated system below: SPECIAL CLAUSE The organization is not profitable organization, it\u2019s meant to provide sound and effective education, skills and awareness to the community and also promote health and peace within the community. ARTICLE ONE: AIMS AND OBJECTIVES The aims and objectives of the association are: 1. To create safe school learning environment and to provide genuine education to its students. 2. To alleviate or reduce congestion in school 3. Promote Healthy Environment within the community 4. To Provide\/enhance discipline and respect in the area 5. Create awareness on the importance of education to the community 6. To engage in programmes and activities aimed at promoting and protecting students 7. To source for support services for students who by any means cannot afford to cater for their education. 8. To assist intellectual and technical advice where necessary 9. Encourage skills acquisitions among the students and promote the students education in our society 10. And also to provide\/enhance the security of the area VISION STATEMENT Ensuring the well-being and advancement of our community. Recognizing that extending assistance and support to others is fundamental to human nature, regardless of religious affiliation. This principle is embraced and enacted by individuals of diverse faiths and beliefs. MISSION STATEMENT Promoting constructive transformation in rural communities and empowering disadvantaged groups to achieve their life aspirations and values through the provision of top-tier healthcare services. Compassionate, Quality-Driven Action. CORE VALUES Teamwork \u2013 Accountability \u2013 Dependability \u2013 Adaptability - Gender Modesty T-A-D-A-G ARTICLE TWO MEMBERSHIP a. Membership shall be open to any community\u2019s member(s) who has interest in providing his quota of contribution toward achieving the set target. b. All members can be from anywhere within the area c. Membership will be after due application by the prospective member organization, a subsequent recommendation by the chairman and an approval by the board of trustees. d. Unlimited members ARTICLE THREE THE EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE A. The Executive Committee (hereinafter referred to as the EXCO) which shall be responsible for the day to day administration of the ASSOCIATION. B. The Executive Committee shall consist of: 1. Three (3) representatives nominated from each axis to be elected as the EXCO of the association. 2. The representative shall come from the four areas. They are: - Dutsin-Amare - Kerau - Kofar Marusa - U\/Dantura C. Discussion A simple majority members present at any meeting shall carry decisions of the EXCO Except on issues of constitutional amendment, where a two-third majority will be required. D. Officers The EXCO shall consist of the following officers: 1. Chairman CEO The chairman shall preside in all general and executive meeting and shall be responsible for the proper conduct of the business at all such meetings. The chairman shall sign the minutes of all meetings except the meetings of special committee that he is not a chairman. The chairman shall be the signatory to the account; the chairman shall have a casting vote. 2. Vice Chairman The Vice Chairman shall in the absence of the Chairman take over his duties mentioned. And carry such responsibilities assigned to him by the chairman and also have a casting vote. 3. The Secretary The Secretary shall take or cause to be taken and keep the proper books provided for the purposes, all minutes of the meetings of the association (congress and executive committee). Conduct all correspondents as may require. Prepare the order of business for meeting of all organs of the association with the approval of the coordinator. Supervise the day to day activities of the secretariat of the association. In the event of removal or resignation he shall immediately hand over to the Assistant Secretary of his nominees all records, files, and other property of the association. He shall be the Chief Administration of the Secretariat. 4. Assistant Secretary He shall assist the Secretary in running the day to day activities of the school administration. He shall be assigned to specific functions by the executive committee. He should act as the secretary in the absence of the Secretary. 5. Treasurer Received and promptly pay into the association\u2019s account all monies receive for and on behalf of the association. Keep an impress account as and when authorized by the executive committee. Ensure that proper accounts and records are kept and may cause such books to be produced for inspection at periodic auditing. He should be among the signatories. 6. Assistant Treasurer Assistant Treasurer should take over the responsibilities of the Treasurer, if not available. 7. Financial Secretary The Financial Secretary shall be in custody of all receipts due to organization and shall keep records of all monitory\/financial transactions, and shall be one of the signatories to the accountant. 8. Public Relation Officer I (PROI) The P.R.O shall be responsible for the association information and propaganda services and activities all of which must be consistent with constitution of the organization. 9. Public Relation Officer II (P.R.O II) He shall assist the PRO in conducting his responsibilities and may be assigned with any other function by the Executive Committee and to act in place of PRO. 10. Auditor The Auditor shall be responsible to investigate all financial transactions and other working irregularities. 11. Organizing Secretary\/Coordinator: Schedule and organize events accordingly 12. Welfare Officer: is responsible for the welfare services of the organization. ELECTION\/DURATION OF OFFICE 1. Election of members of the EXCO shall be in a general meeting of all members summon for the purpose. 2. Members of the EXCO shall serve for a period of two (4) years. 3. The appointment of the executives should be through election REMOVAL FROM OFFICE 1. Notwithstanding the provision of (1) above, any member of the EXCO found guilty of an offence involving the revealing secrets of the association shall be removed by two-third majority of the members present at a meeting. 2. The Vacant post (2) affected shall be filled with a member from the location the EXCO comes from through election by simple majority a simple majority vote of the members of the group from where the member is representing. 3. Acts that may necessitate removal from office are set out in the below Article. ARTICLE FOUR FINANCE A. Source of Income: I. Special levies II. Grant from charitable trust and foundations III. Donations IV. Government Grants B. Financial Year Financial year of ASSOCIATION shall follow the calendar year from January, 1st to December, 31st every year. C. Bankers\/Signatories The Bankers\u2019\/Signatories of the school\/association shall be three (3) signatories consisting of: I. The Chairman II. The Secretary III. The Financial Secretary IV. The Treasurer The mandate for withdrawal of funds shall be the chairman plus any of the signatories (any two) D. The books of account of DAKKEDAN shall be available for auditing. ARTICLE FIVE DISCIPLINE A. Suspension Any member of DAKKEDAN that found neglecting his assigned duties all efforts made to adjust his act proved abortive. B. Expulsion Any Member that misappropriates the association funds or found guilty of grave misconduct capable of bringing the association name into disrepute shall be expelled. A member that fails to attend the association meeting or activities three times consecutively or fails to discharge his\/her statutory duty without genuine reasons shall be punished. Any member believes in any manner or any ways detrimental to the interest of the school will lose his membership or work. Any member found revealing the secret\/plans to anybody shall be liable to face the disciplinary committee for judgment. ARTICLE SIX GENERAL PROVISION: Meeting Ad-Hoc Committee Meeting There shall be the meeting of any committee set by the association to deal with any matter with specific term of reference whenever necessary. Quorum One third of members shall form quorum at any meeting. Decision: ARTICLE SEVEN WINDING UP \uf0d8 If in the event of winding up or dissolution of the association there remain after satisfaction of all its debts and liabilities are settled if any. This is in the case where the association was given a loan or any commitments from any source, the debts and liabilities incurred must be settled by the members, before the disbanding of the association would take place. \uf0d8 Untiring crisis which rock the association and all effort made to stop it proved abortive, the association could be wind-up for peace to rein and the above phase is covered. ARTICLE EIGHT AMENDMENT The provision of this constitution is subject to review and amendment from time to time as when necessary. The draft of such amendment discuss in the two consecutive meetings of the coordinating committee, before the approval of the two-third majority of members present at the general meeting. ARTICLE NINE SUPREMECY OF THE CONSTITUTION The constitution shall be the supreme document of the association and shall overrule, supersede any by law or regulation made by an Ad-hoc or standing committee for any purpose with regard to the progress of the community. Activity Reports The activity reports are based on task performed by the association monthly, quarterly or yearly. The activities that were performed by the association within the period under review that is from 2021 \u2013 2023 are detailed below: i. Free Students enrollments of various categories of new entry level and continuing students ii. Staff training on how to develop a curriculum and manage classroom iii. INTERNAL CONTROL SYSTEM DAKKEDAN operates a robust and transparent Internal Financial Control System. The organization maintains an account with FCMB in its name. Financial requests originate from project staff and are processed by the organization\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\'s accounts officers before being submitted to the Executive Director for approval, provided they do not exceed a predetermined threshold. Any request surpassing this threshold must gain approval from the Management Board. The approval and disbursement of funds follow the same hierarchical channel. DAKKEDAN retains an external auditor to conduct annual or biennial audits, as necessary. All financial activities adhere to a comprehensive organizational financial policy, which undergoes periodic updates to accommodate organizational changes and advancements. DAKKEDAN CODE OF CONDUCT POLICIES 2023 Organizations typically implement a range of mechanisms to safeguard against the violation and abuse of beneficiaries, including sexual exploitation and abuse. Some common strategies include: I. Strict Codes of Conduct: Organizations establish clear and comprehensive codes of conduct that outline expected behaviors and explicitly prohibit any form of exploitation or abuse. II. Training and Capacity Building: Staff and volunteers receive regular training on safeguarding policies, procedures, and relevant laws. This training emphasizes recognizing, preventing, and reporting any signs of abuse. III. Recruitment and Vetting Processes: Robust recruitment processes include thorough background checks and screening of staff and volunteers to ensure they do not pose a risk to beneficiaries. IV. Reporting Mechanisms: Organizations set up confidential and accessible reporting mechanisms for beneficiaries, staff, and other stakeholders to report any concerns or incidents of abuse. V. Whistleblower Protection: Policies are in place to protect individuals who report concerns or incidents of abuse from retaliation or victimization. VI. Accountability and Investigation: Organizations have clear procedures for investigating allegations of abuse, including appointing independent investigators and taking appropriate disciplinary action when necessary. VII. Community Engagement: Engaging with local communities and beneficiaries helps build trust and awareness around safeguarding issues and encourages reporting of any concerns. VIII. Monitoring and Evaluation: Regular monitoring and evaluation of safeguarding measures ensure that policies and procedures are effective and continuously improved. By implementing these mechanisms, organizations strive to create safe environments for their beneficiaries and prevent any form of exploitation or abuse. Organizations employ various mechanisms to safeguard against fraud, corruption, and other unethical behavior: 1. Policies and Procedures: Establishing clear policies and procedures that outline expected ethical conduct, financial controls, and reporting mechanisms for suspected fraud or corruption. 2. Internal Controls: Implementing internal controls such as segregation of duties, authorization processes, and regular audits to detect and prevent fraudulent activities. 3. Code of Ethics: Enforcing a code of ethics that articulates the organization\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\'s values and expectations regarding honesty, integrity, and transparency in all operations. 4. Training and Awareness: Providing regular training and awareness programs to staff and stakeholders on fraud prevention, ethical decision-making, and the consequences of engaging in unethical behavior. 5. Whistleblower Protection: Offering protection to whistleblowers who report suspected fraud or corruption, ensuring confidentiality and safeguarding against retaliation. 6. Due Diligence in Partnerships: Conducting thorough due diligence on partners, suppliers, and contractors to mitigate the risk of collusion or involvement in fraudulent activities. 7. Transparent Financial Practices: Maintaining transparent financial reporting and accountability mechanisms to ensure funds are used appropriately and in accordance with donor requirements. 8. Independent Oversight: Establishing independent oversight bodies such as audit committees or ombudspersons to provide impartial review and oversight of organizational activities. By implementing these mechanisms, organizations aim to promote a culture of integrity, accountability, and transparency, reducing the likelihood of fraud, corruption, and unethical behavior.","annualReports":"DAKKEDAN Education Development and Humanitarian Support Support Association Annual Report for year 2024: Though the association was incorporated in the year 2023 but was in existence since 2015, its annual report will be minimal , the vision of the NGO is Ensuring the well-being and advancement of our community. Recognizing that extending assistance and support to others is fundamental to human nature, regardless of religious affiliation. This principle is embraced and enacted by individuals of diverse faiths and beliefs. and its mission is Promoting constructive transformation in rural communities and empowering disadvantaged groups to achieve their life aspirations and values through the provision of top-tier healthcare services. Compassionate, Quality-Driven Action. Overview of the Activities: Education Initiatives - The NGO have educative, skills and well experience staff with higher educational qualification ranging from PhD. to Degree, there are many initiatives explored to develop the educational activities within the community. The association is the founder Lawal Danhalilu Memorial Community Day Secondary School, with a total number of 600 students both senior and junior classes, The NGO contributes a lot in helping less privilege people, especially girls. The NGO concentrate more on girls child education it gives scholarship to girls that could not afford to go to school. With regards to humanitarian support program the NGO is not left behind as its partake in community development project such as providing the community with borehole that feeds the community with good water, give aids to people affected with floods, banditry, hunger and disease. Changes has been achieved in the association as its change the educational turnover within the community, as before there are less girls attending school but bow the population has rise and youth were given skills acquisition training for self development and sustainability. The NGO achieve a lot in its activities as it provide the community with school that could be afford by less privilege people and also sponsor girls to have good and qualitative education, we constructed a borehole that provide healthy water to the community, people with disability are given special attention to see that they are carried along. Trainings and workshops to teachers were given in the filed of classroom management, curriculum development etc. so as to pass qualitative education to the students. The Financial summary of the association will be provided and the end of the year. The association had partnership and collaboration with other NGOs in the community like SACCEFA , ACOMIN, NACTAL etc. We have attended workshops win awards from some NGOs. The organization have some challenges in financial raising and community acceptance due to the movement involve girls child education, gathering youth also is one of the challenges faced because most of them want to hear money given programs, but due to our courage and optimism the challenges were overcome and have the cooperation of the community members and youth. We are now operating our activities smoothly with direction. We are now looking ahead to achieve more in our goals and objectives.","auditedAccounts":"The Association was officially incorporated with federal government in the year 2023 and its audited account is going to be officially audited at the end of this year 2024.","keyEvaluations":"Formation Evaluations are well structured to suit the movement of the NGO. 2. org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Linkgates for Humanitarian and Development Initiative (LiGHuD) Annual Strategic Plan 2025 ________________________________________ Executive Summary This strategic plan outlines Linkgates for Humanitarian and Development Initiative\\'s (LiGHuD) vision, goals, and priorities for 2025. Our focus will be on fostering sustainable development through targeted interventions in education, health, economic empowerment, and environmental sustainability. Guided by our mission, this plan aims to enhance organizational impact, strengthen partnerships, and ensure the effective utilization of resources. ________________________________________ Vision and Mission Vision: To be a leading catalyst for positive social change, empowering communities to achieve sustainable development and resilience. Mission: To enhance the quality of life in underserved communities through inclusive, innovative, and impactful programs in health, education, livelihood, and environmental sustainability. ________________________________________ Strategic Objectives for 2025 Objective 1: Strengthen Organizational Capacity \u2022 Action Steps: o Conduct capacity-building training for staff and volunteers. o Enhance financial management and reporting systems to align with international standards. o Develop a robust Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability, and Learning (MEAL) framework. \u2022 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): o 80% of staff trained in project management and technical skills. o Implementation of a MEAL framework in all projects by Q3. o Quarterly financial audits completed. Objective 2: Expand Programmatic Reach \u2022 Action Steps: o Scale up interventions in multipurpose cash transfer (MPC) and Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene (WASH). o Initiate three new projects in disaster-affected regions focusing on livelihoods and health. o Partner with local governments and international organizations to secure funding for new initiatives. \u2022 KPIs: o 20% increase in beneficiaries served compared to 2024. o Successful implementation of at least three new projects. o Partnership agreements signed with three new international agencies. Objective 3: Foster Community Engagement and Ownership \u2022 Action Steps: o Establish community advisory boards for all major projects. o Conduct monthly town hall meetings to gather feedback and raise awareness. o Launch community-driven initiatives in climate-smart agriculture. \u2022 KPIs: o 90% of projects with active community advisory boards. o 12 town hall meetings conducted annually. o 5 new climate-smart agriculture initiatives launched. Objective 4: Advocate for Policy Change \u2022 Action Steps: o Develop advocacy campaigns focusing on education, health, and climate change. o Engage policymakers through forums and policy briefs. o Collaborate with other NGOs to amplify advocacy efforts. \u2022 KPIs: o Three policy briefs submitted to state and federal governments. o Participation in at least five advocacy forums. o One policy change influenced through advocacy efforts. Objective 5: Strengthen Resource Mobilization \u2022 Action Steps: o Diversify funding streams by applying for international grants and partnering with private sector organizations. o Organize annual fundraising events and campaigns. o Establish a donor management system to enhance communication and retention. \u2022 KPIs: o 30% increase in funding compared to 2024. o Three new grant applications submitted. o 85% donor retention rate achieved. ________________________________________ Key Focus Areas 1. Health and Nutrition \u2022 Improve access to quality healthcare and nutrition for vulnerable populations. \u2022 Launch maternal and child health programs in underserved areas. 2. Education and Skills Development \u2022 Provide scholarships and learning materials for underprivileged children. \u2022 Establish vocational training centers for youth and women. 3. Economic Empowerment \u2022 Facilitate access to microfinance for small-scale entrepreneurs. \u2022 Conduct financial literacy and business development training. 4. Environmental Sustainability \u2022 Promote reforestation and clean energy solutions. \u2022 Implement waste management and WASH programs to improve public health. ________________________________________ Monitoring and Evaluation LiGHuD will implement a comprehensive MEAL framework to track progress and assess the impact of its programs. Key activities include: \u2022 Baseline and end-line surveys for all major projects. \u2022 Monthly performance reviews. \u2022 Annual impact assessment reports. ________________________________________ Budget Overview \u2022 Projected Income: NGN 250 million \u2022 Projected Expenditure: NGN 230 million o Programmatic Costs: 70% o Administrative Costs: 20% o Fundraising Costs: 10% ________________________________________ Conclusion This strategic plan serves as a roadmap for LiGHuD to achieve its mission and maximize its impact in 2025. By focusing on organizational growth, program expansion, and community engagement, we aim to empower communities and drive sustainable development. We look forward to collaborating with partners, donors, and stakeholders to turn this vision into reality. ________________________________________ Approved by: Umar Isah Executive Director Linkgates for Humanitarian and Development Initiative Date: 24 December 2024","annualReports":"Linkgates for Humanitarian & Development Initiative (LiGHuD) 2024 Annual Report Message from the Executive Director Dear Stakeholders, It is with immense pride and gratitude that I present the 2024 Annual Report for Linkgates for Humanitarian & Development Initiative (LiGHuD). This year has been transformative, marked by impactful projects, strategic partnerships, and significant progress toward our mission of fostering sustainable development and resilience in vulnerable communities. Your unwavering support has been the cornerstone of our success. Warm regards, Umar Isah Executive Director, LiGHuD About Linkgates LiGHuD is dedicated to addressing humanitarian and developmental challenges through innovative and inclusive approaches. Operating across Nigeria, our initiatives prioritize environmental sustainability, economic empowerment, social cohesion, and emergency response. We align our programs with global standards and frameworks, including the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). 2024 Achievements at a Glance \u2022 Community Engagement: Reached over 50,000 beneficiaries across 20 communities. \u2022 Climate Action: Planted 10,000 trees through reforestation campaigns in Sokoto and Kebbi states. \u2022 Economic Empowerment: Trained and supported 2,500 women and youth with vocational skills and startup kits. \u2022 Emergency Response: Provided food, non-food items, and hygiene kits to 5,000 flood-affected households. \u2022 Advocacy and Awareness: Conducted 15 sensitization programs on preventing violent extremism, reaching over 10,000 participants. Key Programs and Impact 1. Environmental Sustainability \u2022 Project: Great Green Wall Campaign \u2022 Impact: Planted 10,000 seedlings, rehabilitated 20 hectares of degraded land, and engaged local farmers in climate-smart agriculture practices. 2. Economic Empowerment \u2022 Project: Skills for Change Initiative \u2022 Impact: Empowered 1,500 women and 1,000 youth with skills in tailoring, soap making, and agricultural value chains. Distributed startup materials to 85% of participants, ensuring sustained livelihoods. 3. Emergency and Humanitarian Assistance \u2022 Project: Flood Response in Kebbi State \u2022 Impact: Distributed cash transfers and essential supplies to 5,000 flood-affected households, ensuring access to food, shelter, and hygiene facilities. 4. Social Cohesion and Advocacy \u2022 Project: Partnership Against Violent Extremism (PAVE) \u2022 Impact: Conducted community dialogues, youth forums, and leadership training programs to promote peace and reduce violence in conflict-prone areas. Financial Overview Revenue Sources Amount (NGN) Grants and Donations 420,000 Partnerships and Sponsorships 200,000 In-Kind Contributions 170,000 Total Revenue 790,000, Expenditures Amount (NGN) Program Implementation 360,000 Administrative Costs 120,000, Capacity Building 195,000 Advocacy and Outreach 115,000, Total Expenditures 790,000 ________________________________________ Partnerships In 2024, LiGHuD collaborated with the following organizations: \u2022 Danish Refugees Council (DRC): Supported in data profiling of flood disaster victims . \u2022 National Emergency Management Agency (NEMA): Partnered for disaster response and recovery efforts. \u2022 Partnership Against Violent Extremism (PAVE): Enhanced community resilience through peace-building programs. Challenges and Lessons Learned Despite our successes, LiGHuD faced challenges, including limited funding for certain projects and logistical difficulties in hard-to-reach areas. These experiences underscored the importance of strategic planning, local partnerships, and adaptive programming. Looking Ahead In 2025, LiGHuD aims to: \u2022 Expand climate action initiatives to 10 additional communities. \u2022 Scale up vocational training programs, targeting 5,000 beneficiaries. \u2022 Enhance our emergency response capacity with new technologies and partnerships. Acknowledgments We extend our heartfelt gratitude to our donors, partners, and community members who have supported our mission. Together, we are making a tangible difference in the lives of thousands. For more information, please contact: Umar Isah Executive Director, LiGHuD Email: linkgateforhumanity@gmail.com","auditedAccounts":"linkgateforhumanity@gmail.com","keyEvaluations":"women empowerment program in wamako LGA Sokoto state, wash program wurno and tambuwal LGAS sokototo tree plantation program sokoto south ,sokoto north ,wurno ,sabon birni and tambuwal LGAS of sokoto state"}

Peace-building program: Engaging traditional and religious leaders for peace, as well as cultural activities promoting peace and social inclusion. 2. Assistances and protection programs: These is covering health, nutrition, WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene), education, gender-based violence (GBV), gender inclusion, livelihood support, mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS), and child protection. 3. Advocacy on Human Security (Governance): Advocacy on Human Security Program, advocating for policies and actions that enhance human security, viz: 1) economic security (e.g., freedom from poverty); (2) food security (e.g., access to food); (3) health security (e.g., access to health care and protection from diseases); (4) environmental security (e.g., protection from such dangers as environmental pollution and depletion); (5) personal security (e.g., physical safety from such things as torture, war, criminal attacks, domestic violence, drug use, suicide, and even traffic accidents); (6) community security (e.g., survival of traditional cultures and ethnic groups as well as the physical security of these groups); and (7) political security (e.g., enjoyment of civil and political rights, and freedom from political oppression). 4. Family Connection Program: Featuring inspired stories and empowerment of individuals aimed at strengthening family bonds and individual empowerment 5. School program: Establishment of cultural clubs in Schools, advocating for drug abuse prevention, GBV awareness, climate action, and social inclusion among students. 6. The Lost sheep: Here we intend to focus on the rehabilitation and reintegration of individuals affected by drug abuse and other social vices"}

A5:2, Fourth floor, Block A wing, Plot 1387 Aminu Kano Crescent Wuse II, Abuja.","emailAddress":"emppalieveinitiative@gmail.com","facebook":"","twitter":"","linkedIn":"","orgMission":"To improve the health and development of women and children and contribute to national and UN global goals for sustainable development.","orgVision":"To empower women and children in Nigeria through improved health, education, and sustainable development.","website":"www.emppalieveinitiative.org","thematicAreas":["Health"]}
org_inst_docs: {"strategicPlans":"Health & Well-being: Expand maternal and child healthcare, nutrition programs, and reproductive health education. Education & Capacity Building: Provide scholarships, vocational training, and literacy programs for women and children. Economic Empowerment: Support women\u2019s cooperatives, microfinance programs, and entrepreneurial training. Advocacy & Policy Engagement: Promote women and children\u2019s rights, collaborate with government agencies, and influence policies. Implementation Strategy: Strengthen partnerships with NGOs, government agencies, and private organizations. Secure funding through grants, donor support, and fundraising campaigns. Track progress using Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for program effectiveness.","annualReports":"Message from the Chairman As the Chairman of Emppalieve Initiative, I am proud to present our annual report for 2023, which highlights the significant progress we have made in empowering women and children across Nigeria. This year has been a testament to our collective efforts, resilience, and unwavering commitment to improving the lives of vulnerable groups in our society. Our mission to foster gender equality, enhance health and education, and support economic independence has remained at the core of everything we do. Together with our partners, volunteers, and communities, we have made measurable strides in achieving our vision of a society where every woman and child can thrive. We are grateful for the continued support of our stakeholders, and we look forward to building on this success in the coming year. Dr. Ajaru Antonia Chinwe Chairman, Board of Directors About Emppalieve Initiative Emppalieve Initiative for the Empowerment of Women and Children in Nigeria (EIEWC) is a Non-Governmental Organization (NGO) dedicated to empowering women and children to live healthy, independent, and successful lives. Established in 2012, we work to combat poverty, improve education, promote gender equality, and provide access to healthcare services, all while fostering a sense of dignity and self-worth. Our work focuses on: Empowering women to achieve economic independence and self-sufficiency. Ensuring children have access to quality education and healthcare. Promoting community development and supporting vulnerable populations. Key Achievements of 2023 1. Support for Education: Paid school fees for over 200 indigent children, ensuring they continue their education without interruption. Provided educational materials to over 500 students in underserved communities. 2. Skill Development and Economic Empowerment: Built and equipped a Skill Acquisition Centre in Benue State, providing vocational training for over 150 women and youth in tailoring, soap-making, and small-scale business development. Donated 2 motorcycles to men in Imo State, enabling them to engage in livelihood-enhancing activities and reducing unemployment. 3. Healthcare and Sanitation: Constructed and furnished a Primary Healthcare Centre in Gombe State, providing access to maternal and child healthcare services for over 2,000 residents. Distributed 5,000 mosquito-treated nets and sanitation supplies to reduce the spread of malaria and improve hygiene in vulnerable communities. 4. Humanitarian Assistance: Distributed 2,000 bags of rice to women in Nasarawa State, supporting families and alleviating hunger. Delivered 3,000 cooked meals and 5,000 food packages to the less-privileged in Abuja and Nasarawa during the pandemic, providing much-needed relief to vulnerable populations. 5. Pandemic Response and Health Supplies: Delivered 10,000 face masks (N95), 7,000 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) kits, 6,000 RDTs, and 5,000 ACTs to communities, ensuring they were equipped to fight COVID-19. Collaborated with government agencies to strengthen preparedness for future health crises. 6. Social Outreach and Prison Support: Conducted outreach programs at Kuje Correctional Centre, providing moral and material support to inmates and advocating for their rights. Impact and Testimonials Our efforts have had a lasting impact on the lives of women, children, and entire communities. Below are some quotes from the people we\u2019ve served: \u2022 \u201cI was able to learn tailoring and now I can support my family. E Emppalieve Initiative has changed my life.\u201d Mary, Skill Acquisition Centre graduate \u2022 \u201cThanks to the school fee payment, my son has settled his final year school fees, we are glad Emppalieve Initiative came through for us.\u201d John, father of an education beneficiary Challenges and Future Plans While we have achieved a lot this year, we have also faced challenges, particularly in mobilizing sufficient resources to meet the growing needs of the communities we serve. The COVID-19 pandemic strained our resources and affected the delivery of some of our programs. For the Coming Year, We Plan To: Expand our Skill Acquisition Centres to reach more women and youth in rural areas. Increase the number of healthcare centers we support, particularly in underserved regions. Launch a women\u2019s empowerment fund to help women start and sustain small businesses. We are immensely proud of our achievements this year, but there is still much work to be done. We invite our donors, partners, and stakeholders to continue supporting us in our mission to empower women and children and build a brighter future for all.","auditedAccounts":"https:\/\/drive.google.com\/file\/d\/1-z1s7xWg5-QYp78BwM6utv5UJ_Dm45Lz\/view?usp=sharing and https:\/\/drive.google.com\/file\/d\/1G1CcSlEmdAm46YLHd45uNW2TjfI6itxO\/view?usp=sharing","keyEvaluations":"https:\/\/drive.google.com\/drive\/folders\/1GvsUjbggRvPyx7W4w0c4HYM2Cx-gvvrt?usp=sharing"}
